topics.title: Howard Stern's Letterman Appearance and CBS Lawsuit
topics.summary: The show features a discussion regarding Howard Stern's appearance on David Letterman, the subsequent lack of promotion by CBS, and his ongoing legal dispute with former executive Leslie Moonves.

topics.title: Legal Battle with CBS and Late Night Appearances
topics.summary: The studio discusses the legal fallout from a lawsuit filed by Leslie Moonves, the host's appearance on the Late Show with David Letterman, and the ongoing competition with rival radio personalities.

topics.title: Legal Battles and Promotional Merchandise
topics.summary: The studio discusses a lawsuit against CBS, the success of promotional t-shirts, and various topics including personal anecdotes and upcoming roast events.

topics.title: Legal Dispute and Media Appearances
topics.summary: The studio discusses a recent television appearance, the details of an ongoing breach of contract lawsuit against Howard Stern by CBS, and various cultural topics including hunting shows and baking.

topics.title: Rock and Roll Hall of Fame Experience
topics.summary: The studio recounts a reporter's tense experience being escorted out by security at the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame due to concerns over the Howard Stern brand.

topics.title: Radio Show Segment and Guest Interactions
topics.summary: The show features a series of segments including listener interactions, discussions about past comedy bits, a call from an attorney regarding media litigation, and the introduction of upcoming guests.

topics.title: Cosmo Channel and Studio Events
topics.summary: The studio explores the launch of a new Cosmo radio channel, discusses a guest's modeling background, and reacts to an incident involving a staff member streaking.

topics.title: Late Night Talk Show Experience
topics.summary: The studio discusses a recent appearance on the Late Show, the atmosphere backstage at CBS, and the performance of Carrie Underwood.

topics.title: Comedy Roast and Guest Interviews
topics.summary: The studio features a series of segments including discussions on music preferences, upcoming comedy roasts, listener interactions regarding trivia and snacks, and an introduction to a professional submissive's memoir.

topics.title: Interview with Professional Submissive Joan Kelly
topics.summary: The studio hosts an interview with Joan Kelly, a professional submissive who discusses her experiences with extreme fetishes, including being spanked for hours and having her vagina sewn shut.

topics.title: Book Promotion and Sexual Performance
topics.summary: The studio features a guest who discusses her book while participating in a live sexual performance segment involving spanking, self-pleasure, and interaction with the hosts.

topics.title: Radio Industry Regulations and Satellite Competition
topics.summary: The studio discusses the National Association of Broadcasters' efforts to lobby for stricter regulations on satellite radio to level the playing field against terrestrial broadcasting.

topics.title: Analysis of a Mean-Spirited Letter
topics.summary: The studio explores the impact of a poorly worded letter from a friend regarding an ongoing lawsuit, debating whether the sender intended to be discouraging or was simply sharing a harsh personal experience.

topics.title: Legal Risks and Media Commentary
topics.summary: The studio explores the risks of litigation, the nuances of a legal warning letter, and various news stories including criminal rights, a missing meditator, and high-profile book advances.

topics.title: Media Appearances and Celebrity News
topics.summary: The studio discusses a guest's experience on a television network, the legal troubles of various public figures including Michael Jackson, and recent news regarding South Park.

topics.title: Isaac Hayes and South Park Controversy
topics.summary: The show discusses Isaac Hayes' request to be released from his contract with South Park following the show's portrayal of Scientology.

transcript.text: Hey now, good morning everybody. Welcome to the show.
And here we go again.
Good morning!
Good morning, Robin.
I saw you on Letterman.
Yes, yes, I was on Letterman for quite a time yesterday.
Yeah, three segments.
Yeah, CBS finally blinked and issued a statement saying that I'm desperate.
Did you read that in the paper today?
No.
They said that...
You know, I got something to say about that too, because I fell asleep.
I was so pissed off of myself that I woke up today and I heard a lot of the replay on CBS News, actually.
They played a lot of what you said, and it sounded really funny.
But you know, I watched that watching Ellie last night, the Julia Louis-Dreyfus thing,
because it got insanely good reviews, and I was like, "Let me see what this looks like."
They promoed Letterman, did not promo you.
Yeah, that was one of the observations I was going to make.
Yeah, they didn't mention you.
They didn't promo me, and the fact of the matter is, most people probably didn't even know I was on.
Well, how weird is that? I'm not weird.
We have a smaller audience on satellite. I think it was a bonehead move, but Leslie Moonves wasn't going to sit and promote me bashing him.
Well, he would rather see a show fail.
Right.
I mean, obviously, I know why it happened, but it was just like, "Well, did you see Letterman and Carrie Underwood?"
And you're like, "Okay, what the... I'm not saying..."
What kind of show is that?
Right, I'm not saying up to 11.30.
That's them blinking. When they say it doesn't bother them, you know it bothers them.
Yeah, you know, it's funny you say that, because I mean, I don't watch enough TV on Sunday.
But our buddy Ross was watching all the NCAA stuff on Sunday, and he said that they weren't even...
Usually on Sundays, they promote the hell out of Letterman, and he didn't even see Letterman promos at all.
Right.
So they didn't promote it, they tried to keep it quiet, and then they issued a statement.
They must have issued this statement even before I was done.
You know, and I know there must have been some kind of weird discussions going on after I taped the show, because...
Usually Vinny is right on the phone to me afterwards, you know, applauding my appearance, or thanking me for doing it.
Did you even see him?
I did see him for two seconds, and he just ran out of there. You know, poor Vinny.
He didn't, you know, listen, he gets a paycheck from Leslie Moonves.
He doesn't know what to do.
He doesn't know what to do, but I emmed him. His instant message was on last night.
I emmed him 50 times, and he didn't get back to me, which led me to believe they were debating either editing the thing or...
They didn't know what they were going to do.
Yeah, I mean, they didn't promote it.
Well, when I saw you not in the promo, I said, I was actually on the phone with Dana, and I said, "Fuck, maybe they totally cut him out."
Well, then they would have really looked bad. Either way, they looked bad.
And, you know, the weird thing is, as you point out, CBS Radio, WINS, they cover it, because they kind of have to cover it.
They don't want to seem like they're biased, but...
They played quite a bit of your segment.
They almost go overboard to make sure that they're proper.
Well, yeah, they're trying, and they'll always announce, "Well, this company is owned by..."
Right, right.
Here, CBS calls Howard desperate. That's right. I am desperate. I'm up against a huge corporation. It's me against them.
Why are you talking desperate? You're fighting CBS.
I'm desperate. You know, the only thing I got in this battle is that I can get a letterman, that I can go public with this thing.
That's something Leslie Moonves didn't really think about.
Leslie Moonves hasn't dipped into his pocket. I'm paying lawyers up to ying yang to defend this thing.
Yeah, let CBS come after less and see what he does.
Yeah, we'll see how desperate he is. I'm desperate, because I'm out there trying to shine a light on these cockroaches.
You're a U.S. citizen getting bullied around.
CBS brass quickly slammed Howard Stern last night following his vitriolic...
Vitriolic.
...appearance on David Letterman's Late Show.
I'm glad you know what you know the English language. Vitrola. Isn't that the thing you play records on?
Right.
How do you say that? Vitriolic?
Vitriolic.
I'm glad you corrected him, Robin, because I was about to.
We believe his appearance was his desperate attempt to distract attention. See, that's a play on...
Distract attention?
Because I've been saying they've been trying to distract attention from the radio division.
What are you distracting attention from?
We believe his appearance was his desperate attempt to distract attention from the facts of the case.
No, my attempt is to shine a light on the facts of the case after you guys slandered me and bullied me in your lawsuit.
If you didn't want anybody to think about the case, you would not talk about it.
CBS officials said in a surprisingly harsh statement...
The scathing interview, which Stern called CBS boss Les Moonves a bully,
was in response to Moonves and CBS slapping him with a breach of contract lawsuit.
You mean they file a 42-page document calling me essentially a fraud and a crook?
And, you know, saying they want 500 million dollars.
And I'm desperate, yeah. I would say so. I would say I'm desperate.
Like I said, if they were to win this lawsuit, you would be ruined.
Yeah. I'm desperate to shine a light on the cockroaches that are CBS,
who just filed the most frivolous lawsuit in the once great CBS history that they held.
Now they're filing lawsuits against radio dish jockeys.
Just guys, yeah.
Yeah.
In it, they... Then they go through what they accused me of, which you've heard a million times.
On Letterman, Stern blasted Moonves in a rant that lasted through several commercial breaks.
CBS took a third-rate Love Boat actor, made him CEO.
Stern said, mocking his former boss.
Stern sat for the interview wearing a t-shirt, bearing images of Moonves and his wife.
And that was some red dress. I had never seen the picture.
Yeah. That was a nice dress, right?
That's a beautiful dress.
Stern says the lawsuit is Moonves has been dead against him
because the ratings for CBS radio division collapsed.
I think this is Leslie Moonves' way of trying to increase the bottom line, too.
I mean, I don't think he has a real good idea on how to make that radio division profitable.
Yeah, but nobody's going to be listening to the radio division because of this suit, Leslie.
Hey, Pud, you're on the air.
Pud's in New York. Go ahead, Pud.
Hey, hey, Howard. I just wondered, you made a comment last night.
By the way, a great appearance last night. You got a three-segment.
You bounced through two commercials. But I want to know,
you made a comment last night that Leslie Moonves handed you this lawsuit,
42 pages, and he spent an hour reviewing it.
That is correct.
Do you know if he got past the front page?
I don't think so. I don't even think he knew what he was filing
because when he was describing why he was suing me,
at one point he said to me in our meeting, "I'm suing you because
we're not making any money right now."
And I said, "Well, that really has nothing to do with me."
And then he went, "Oh, no, no, no, no. We're suing you because you got a bonus."
I said, "I didn't get a bonus. I got an accelerated payment."
You understand the difference? He goes, "Oh, oh, oh, right. I do.
If that's the case, then that's off the table."
And then they just filed a lawsuit. It's crazy.
Guy doesn't even understand the lawsuit. That's why I said on Letterman,
"Bring Leslie out here to debate the merits of the lawsuit. I guarantee you."
He didn't even know what the lawsuit says.
He'd be at a loss because he didn't probably have asked the first page.
If I could debate him on Letterman, I guarantee you this case, it would be a total joke.
He's not in charge of that. He doesn't even know what he filed.
And now he's sitting there going, "What the hell did I get myself into?"
Every expert is saying that the lawsuit has no merit.
He's such an idiot, this guy. He's such a lightweight.
I can't even begin to explain it to you. To sit with him is to know him.
To know what a lightweight is running that. It's so funny to me who gets to run a corporation.
It's so funny.
Guy's got no balls.
None. Come on, Letterman. I said to him, "Come on. I'm desperate. You're desperate. Let's go."
Well, judging from the fact that they didn't promote this and I doubt if he was going to do that.
Well, I got a great story coming up for you. I'm not going to tell you about how Leslie
Moonves... There was a huge article coming out in Hollywood about a very positive article about me
and the lawsuit and how ridiculous it is. Leslie Moonves had it pulled.
Yeah, you said... Did you say something about that yesterday?
Yeah. I said it to Howard 100 News.
Yeah, I heard that and I was like, "Oh, I wonder what publication that is."
I'll tell you all about it in a couple of days. I'm putting it all together.
I'll tell you what else he's up to. There's a lot of things he's up to.
Hey, Howard, one more thing.
If they're so not worried, why are they worried about what a magazine writes?
If they have such merit, you carrying on in the press would only be to their benefit.
Leslie pulled an article and I have the whole story.
First of all, shame on whatever magazine that is. I mean, that's ridiculous.
How do you do that? You said such a bad precedent there.
It's a little disconcerting to me.
Well, let me clue you in, Artie. These magazines are easy to push around.
Well, I mean, it sucks.
There are some magazines that the advertising is paid for by the networks in Hollywood.
That's how you'd get them.
Yeah, but I'll tell you the whole story.
Howard?
Yeah.
The bumper going into the show up here made me run to my PIVA to see if you were still on.
I thought right up to the show that they would cancel you.
They didn't mention you on any of the bumpers between like 10 o'clock and 11.
Well, that's Leslie Moonves.
1130, just carry Underwood.
Yeah, which they bit themselves in the ass because I usually bring great ratings to Letterman.
Absolutely.
But by not promoting it, they won't because certainly in the old days on testicle radio,
I could promote and you guys would, millions of people would hear about it.
Now we're a much smaller audience. We're just starting out on satellite.
I don't know how many people know about it.
Well, they certainly didn't help. They're an interesting organization when it comes to business.
Well, they knew the appearance was strong.
I got the truth out and they weren't really that comfortable with promoting it.
Of course not.
The fact that I was on there, there's only one reason I ended up on Letterman
because the David Letterman and his producer, what's that guy's name?
Rob Barnett.
Rob Barnett were hell bent on having me on and not being controlled by Leslie.
And they booked it and they went and told him that it was booked since January, which was a lie.
I booked it a week ago.
And Leslie's afraid of them.
Leslie would have looked like an ass if he went up against Letterman, said you can't have Howard.
Well, he's had a disgruntled Letterman before and he doesn't want that.
That's not good for him.
Because then he'd have you and Letterman.
Right, teamed up against him.
He's afraid of that.
Howard, how come he wasted five minutes talking about the plastic surgery?
I mean, he really threw you a curve.
No, not at all.
I was happy to talk about it.
I thought there was some humor in it.
You know, I didn't mind that.
He can't all be about the lawsuit.
I understand the guy's doing a show.
For me, the sad part for me is that I really just want to go on there and bash Leslie Moonves
and tell people what this lawsuit's all about.
I feel I got to defend my name.
You know, Dave's got to keep the thing humorous.
He's got to, he tried to, you know, certainly, I don't know.
Talk about Leslie in a fair way.
Well, but he did let it get serious for a moment, which I was surprised.
He didn't try to keep the levity going.
He got a little serious there for a little while.
Well, I, yeah, I got, I, that was my doing because I was not, he kept trying to interrupt
me and I just said, no, I want to talk about this.
You know, I don't want to make light of it entirely.
Like, I know you got to keep things light and it's a joke.
But you know what?
Here's the bottom line.
I'm being sued by a huge corporation with endless resources after putting in 20 years
and being told by them, I did a good job right up until the end.
Right.
The absurdity, the absurdity of it is not funny to me.
And, and, and the bottom line is, you know, you got to, you got to defend yourself.
This is crazy.
You know, if you could prove that you got a ratings bump last night,
that is just going to help your case.
The bad part about the appearance was they actually made revenue off of you coming there.
That's okay.
I'm all right with it.
They need all the help they can get.
Well, yeah, they got to pay their lawyers too.
Yeah.
I got, I got something out of it and they got something out of it.
They got revenue and I got, I got to go on and explain myself right on their own damn airwaves.
Well, thank you, Howard.
All right.
Thank you.
Have a good one, man.
Right.

Okay.

Great.
